The area enclosed by the polygon with vertices A(1, 3), B(4, 6), C(7, 2), and D(3, -1) is calculated using the Shoelace Theorem as 19 square units.
To find the shortest route starting and ending at A while visiting B, C, and D exactly once, we evaluate the possible permutations of the landmarks.
The distance between A and B is approximately 4.24 units, while B to C is roughly 5.39 units.
The segment from C to D measures about 5.83 units and D back to A is 4.24 units.
Another possibility for the route involves going A to D first, then to C, then to B, and finally back to A.
Calculating the total distance for the route A-B-C-D-A yields approximately 19.70 units.
The alternative route A-D-C-B-A results in a total distance of approximately 19.70 units as well due to symmetry in this specific configuration.
A third possibility is the route A-B-D-C-A which totals roughly 22.65 units and is therefore not optimal.
The fourth possibility is A-C-B-D-A which totals roughly 22.65 units and is also not optimal.
Thus the shortest possible route is either A-B-C-D-A or A-D-C-B-A with a total distance of 19.70 units.
